name: Jangiri

ingredients.name: Rice flour
ingredients.notes: or cornstarch, if needed, for batter

ingredients.name: Water
ingredients.notes: very little, for grinding, for batter

steps.instruction: Rinse urad dal (and idli rice if using) twice. Soak in water for 1 to 2 hours.
Drain thoroughly and let sit for 5 minutes to remove excess water.
Transfer to a blender or wet grinder. Sprinkle very little water at a time and grind to a thick, smooth paste like medu vada batter.
The batter should be very smooth and light. To test consistency, drop a spoonful in water — it should float.
Transfer to a bowl. Whip the batter with your hand or a wooden spoon in one direction for at least 10 minutes to aerate it.
Add orange food colour and salt. Mix well. If batter is too thin, add 1 tbsp rice flour.
For sugar syrup, take 3 cups sugar and 1 cup water in a wide pan. Bring to a boil.
Cook until half-string consistency — when stretched between fingers, it forms a string but won't stand stiff. Switch off.
Add cardamom, lemon juice, food colour and rose essence. Keep syrup warm.
Fill a piping bag or zip-lock cover with the batter. Cut a small hole at the tip.
Heat oil in a flat-bottomed pan. The oil should be hot but not smoking.
Pipe the batter in circular, flower-like patterns directly into the oil.
Fry until both sides are slightly crisp but colour has not darkened too much. Flip carefully.
Drain and immediately add to the warm sugar syrup. Soak for 3 to 5 minutes per batch.
Remove from syrup and arrange on a plate. Pour remaining syrup over all jangiris.

tips: The batter consistency is crucial — too thin and jangiris won't hold shape; too thick and they won't be soft inside.
Whipping the batter for 10 minutes aerates it, making the jangiri crispy outside and juicy inside.
Use a flat-bottomed pan for frying so the jangiris have space to spread.
Oil temperature is very important — too hot and they will brown quickly without cooking inside; too cool and they will absorb oil.
Sugar syrup should be warm, not hot, when soaking the jangiris.
Lemon juice prevents the syrup from crystallizing.
Use a skewer or wooden chopstick to flip the jangiris gently.
Jangiris stay soft and juicy for many hours when soaked properly.
